findings.summary: The October 1, 2024 and October 2025 versions present the exact same coding rules regarding the distinction between pre-existing and pregnancy-related conditions. Both versions mandate that Chapter 15 codes be used for postpartum complications only if the provider explicitly documents the condition as pregnancy-related. Furthermore, the instruction to assign Z39.0 as the principal diagnosis for routine postpartum care following an outside delivery with no complications is identical in both texts.
findings.detail: October 1, 2024 (Older): Chapter 15 codes may be used as additional codes to identify documented complications of the pregnancy in conjunction with codes in categories O04, O07, and O08. -> October 2025 (New Version): Codes from Chapter 15 should be assigned as additional codes when identifying documented complications of the pregnancy.

findings.summary: The October 1, 2024 and October 2025 versions present the exact same coding logic regarding the assignment of final characters based on provider documentation of trimester or weeks. Both versions mandate that the 'unspecified trimester' code is reserved exclusively for cases where documentation is insufficient and clarification cannot be obtained. The guidelines for applying these rules to pre-existing conditions versus those developing during the encounter remain identical in both texts. There are no substantive differences in the coding rules, only minor phrasing variations in the extracted profiles.
findings.detail: October 1, 2024 (Older): The 'unspecified trimester' code should rarely be used, only when documentation is insufficient and clarification is not possible. -> October 2025 (New Version): The 'unspecified trimester' code is reserved for cases where documentation is insufficient and clarification cannot be obtained.

findings.summary: The October 1, 2024 and October 2025 versions present the exact same criteria for coding clinically significant conditions, prematurity codes (P05/P07), and sequencing rules. The text excerpts are identical up to the point of truncation, indicating no substantive update to the coding logic occurred between these versions.
findings.detail:
October 1, 2024 (Older): Code all clinically significant conditions noted on routine newborn examination -> October 2025 (New Version): Code all clinically significant conditions noted on routine newborn examination
October 1, 2024 (Older): Assign prematurity codes only if explicitly documented by the provider -> October 2025 (New Version): Assign prematurity codes only if explicitly documented by the provider

findings.summary: The older and newer versions contain identical text excerpts and behavioral requirements for assigning O99.31, O99.33, and O99.32 codes for alcohol, tobacco, and drug use. There are no substantive differences in the coding logic, sequencing rules for abuse, or the requirement for secondary codes from F categories between the two versions. The only variations are minor formatting differences in the 'Behaviors' list and the addition of a redundant sentence in the 'Requirements' section of the new version.
findings.detail:
October 1, 2024 (Older): Behaviors list includes 'Assign O99.31 codes for alcohol use during pregnancy or postpartum' -> October 2025 (New Version): Behaviors list includes 'Assign O99.31 codes for alcohol use during pregnancy, childbirth, or puerperium regardless of expectedness.'
October 1, 2024 (Older): Requirements section repeats the documentation requirement for tobacco twice -> October 2025 (New Version): Requirements section adds a specific line for assigning associated current injury and perpetrator codes in abuse cases.

findings.summary: The October 1, 2024 version and the October 2025 version contain identical behavioral instructions regarding the perinatal period definition and the requirement to use perinatal codes for lifelong conditions. The only variation is the addition of a section header in the newer version, which does not alter the underlying coding logic or requirements. Both versions explicitly state that if a condition originates in the perinatal period and continues throughout life, the perinatal code must be used regardless of the patient's age.
findings.detail: October 1, 2024 (Older): Defines the perinatal period and coding rules without a specific sub-header -> October 2025 (New Version): Defines the perinatal period and coding rules under the specific sub-header 'Use of Chapter 16 Codes after the Perinatal Period'.

findings.summary: The older and newer versions contain identical text regarding the sequencing of principal diagnoses for pregnancy complications, including the handling of non-delivery episodes and cesarean deliveries. The only differences are minor formatting adjustments in the extracted profile summaries, such as the removal of the phrase 'not routinely associated with the procedure' in the requirements section of the new version, which does not alter the coding logic. The core instruction that delivery complications are additional diagnoses and admission reasons dictate sequencing remains exactly the same.
findings.detail:
October 1, 2024 (Older): Principal diagnosis must correspond to the complication necessitating the encounter if no delivery occurs. -> October 2025 (New Version): Principal diagnosis must correspond to the complication necessitating the encounter if no delivery occurs.
October 1, 2024 (Older): Cesarean delivery principal diagnosis must be the condition resulting in the procedure if it prompted admission. -> October 2025 (New Version): For cesarean delivery, select the condition resulting in the procedure if it prompted admission.

findings.section: ICD-10 Maternal Fetal Care Coding Rules
findings.summary: The October 1, 2024 and October 2025 guidelines present the exact same coding logic for assigning 7th characters to specific obstetric categories. The rules for using character '0' in cases of single gestation, insufficient documentation, or clinical impossibility are identical in both versions. Furthermore, the strict requirement that codes O35 and O36 are only assigned when a fetal condition modifies maternal management is unchanged. The text excerpts provided for both versions are verbatim identical, indicating no substantive update to the coding behavior.
findings.detail:
October 1, 2024 (Older): Rules for 7th character assignment and O35/O36 criteria are defined -> October 2025 (New Version): Rules for 7th character assignment and O35/O36 criteria are defined identically.
October 1, 2024 (Older): Character '0' assigned for insufficient documentation -> October 2025 (New Version): Character '0' assigned for insufficient documentation with identical wording.

findings.summary: The October 1, 2024 guideline mandates principal diagnosis O98.7- for symptomatic HIV cases and the combination of O98.7- and Z21 for asymptomatic cases, a rule that is identically preserved in the October 2025 version. Both versions explicitly state that Chapter 15 codes take sequencing priority and require the same specific code combinations for both symptomatic and asymptomatic presentations. The only variation is minor wording in the 'Requirements' section, but the core clinical logic and coding instructions remain identical.
findings.detail:
October 1, 2024 (Older): Mandates O98.7- followed by B20 and illness codes for symptomatic cases, and O98.7- with Z21 for asymptomatic cases. -> October 2025 (New Version): Mandates O98.7- followed by illness codes for symptomatic cases, and O98.7- with Z21 for asymptomatic cases.
October 1, 2024 (Older): Explicitly lists 'B20' as a required follow-up code in the raw text. -> October 2025 (New Version): Generalizes the instruction to 'code(s) for the HIV-related illness(es)' without explicitly listing B20 in the excerpt.

findings.summary: The October 1, 2024 and October 2025 guidelines present the exact same clinical criteria, exclusions, and coding hierarchy for subcategory O24.4. Both versions mandate that codes be restricted to O24.4, prohibit concurrent use of other O24 codes, and require selecting the most intensive treatment code (insulin or oral hypoglycemic) when multiple therapies are used. The text excerpts provided for both versions are character-for-character identical, indicating no substantive update to the coding rules.
findings.detail: October 1, 2024 (Older): Codes Z79.4, Z79.84, and Z79.85 should not be assigned with codes from subcategory O24.4. -> October 2025 (New Version): Codes Z79.4, Z79.84, and Z79.85 should not be assigned with codes from subcategory O24.4.

findings.summary: The October 1, 2024 guideline instructs coders to assign category O35 codes for fetal conditions and prohibits Chapter 16 perinatal codes on the mother's record. The October 2025 version contains the exact same instructions, requirements, and raw text excerpts without any logical or behavioral modifications. The only distinction is the metadata timestamp, indicating a static guideline that has not been updated.
findings.detail:
October 1, 2024 (Older): Instructs assignment of O35 codes and prohibition of Chapter 16 codes -> October 2025 (New Version): Instructs assignment of O35 codes and prohibition of Chapter 16 codes
October 1, 2024 (Older): Requires surgery to be performed on the fetus -> October 2025 (New Version): Requires surgery to be performed on the fetus

findings.summary: The October 1, 2024 and October 2025 guidelines present the exact same coding logic for category O09 usage, complication assignment, and the default O80 code. The text excerpts are virtually identical, differing only in minor phrasing such as 'specific clinical scenario' versus 'specific case', which does not alter the coding rules. There is no expansion of scope, reversal of guidance, or clarification of ambiguous rules between the two versions.
findings.detail:
October 1, 2024 (Older): Secondary chapter 15 codes may be used in conjunction with these codes if appropriate. -> October 2025 (New Version): Secondary chapter 15 codes may be used in conjunction with these codes if appropriate.
October 1, 2024 (Older): specific clinical scenario -> October 2025 (New Version): specific case

findings.summary: The older version from October 1, 2024, instructs coders to assign category O24 first followed by Chapter 4 codes for pregnant patients with diabetes. The newer version from October 2025 contains the exact same instruction and raw text excerpt, indicating no alteration in the coding logic or requirements.
findings.detail: October 1, 2024 (Older): Assign category O24 code first for pregnant patients with diabetes -> October 2025 (New Version): Assign category O24 code first for pregnant patients with diabetes

findings.summary: The older version (October 1, 2024) and the newer version (October 2025) contain identical instructions for assigning codes from categories Q00-Q99/QA1, sequencing Z38 for birth admissions, and handling inherent versus additional manifestations. The only observable difference is a minor typo in the code range label (Q00-QA1 in the new version vs Q00-Q99 in the old), but the operational coding logic, requirements, and raw text excerpts are functionally identical.
findings.detail:
October 1, 2024 (Older): Assign codes from categories Q00-Q99 -> October 2025 (New Version): Assign codes from categories Q00-QA1
October 1, 2024 (Older): Codes from categories Q00-Q99 are appropriate whenever the condition is diagnosed by the provider -> October 2025 (New Version): Codes from Chapter 17 may be used throughout the life of the patient if the condition is diagnosed by the provider

findings.section: ICD-10 Coding Guidelines for Post-Abortion Hemorrhage
findings.summary: The older and newer guidelines contain the exact same raw text excerpt and behavioral instructions regarding the assignment of code O04.6 versus the exclusion of O72.1. There are no substantive differences in the clinical criteria, code selection logic, or deprecated code lists between the two versions. The only variations are minor formatting differences in the extracted profile metadata, such as the feature name capitalization and the specific phrasing of the requirements list, which do not alter the coding outcome.
findings.detail:
October 1, 2024 (Older): Feature named 'Coding Guidelines for Post-Abortion Hemorrhage' with requirements listed as 'Condition must be hemorrhage post elective abortion' -> October 2025 (New Version): Feature named 'Coding of Post-Abortion Hemorrhage' with requirements listed as 'Condition must be hemorrhage following elective abortion'
October 1, 2024 (Older): Raw text excerpt includes 'For hemorrhage post elective abortion...' -> October 2025 (New Version): Raw text excerpt includes 'For hemorrhage post elective abortion...'

findings.summary: The older version strictly prohibited using Code O80 if any other Chapter 15 code was needed to describe a current complication, without distinguishing between active and resolved conditions. The newer version clarifies that Code O80 may be used if a prior antepartum complication has resolved before the delivery admission, provided no current complications exist. This distinction allows for more accurate coding of uncomplicated deliveries where previous pregnancy issues have been managed and resolved.
findings.detail:
October 1, 2024 (Older): Code O80 is not to be used if any other code from chapter 15 is needed to describe a current complication of the antenatal, delivery, or postnatal period. -> October 2025 (New Version): Code O80 may be used if the patient had a complication at some point during the pregnancy, but the complication is not present at the time of the admission for delivery.
October 1, 2024 (Older): Implicitly excludes any history of complications requiring Chapter 15 codes. -> October 2025 (New Version): Explicitly permits Code O80 when antepartum complications are resolved prior to delivery admission.

findings.summary: The October 1, 2024 guideline explicitly required the assignment of both Z33.2 and a Z37 code when an attempted termination resulted in a liveborn fetus. The October 2025 version retains this exact instruction without altering the clinical criteria or the specific codes required. The only variation is a minor reduction in the explicit listing of requirements in the extracted profile, but the core coding behavior remains identical.
findings.detail: October 1, 2024 (Older): Assign code Z33.2 and a code from category Z37 for attempted termination resulting in a liveborn fetus -> October 2025 (New Version): Assign code Z33.2 and a code from category Z37 for attempted termination resulting in a liveborn fetus

findings.summary: The core coding logic remains identical in both versions, mandating a default to birth process origin when documentation is unclear and prohibiting Chapter 16 codes for confirmed community-acquired conditions. The specific instruction to refer to guideline I.C.16.h for COVID-19 is present in both texts without alteration. While the 'Requirements' section in the new version adds a minor note about avoiding defaults, the operational behavior described in the raw text excerpts is unchanged.
findings.detail:
October 1, 2024 (Older): Default to birth process origin if documentation is unclear -> October 2025 (New Version): Default to birth process origin if documentation is unclear
October 1, 2024 (Older): Refer to guideline I.C.16.h for COVID-19 -> October 2025 (New Version): Refer to guideline I.C.16.h for COVID-19

findings.summary: The October 1, 2024 guideline instructs coders to assign U07.1 for positive tests without transmission details and P35.8 with U07.1 for in utero transmission, while using Z38 as the principal diagnosis. The October 2025 version contains the exact same instructions, code assignments, and documentation requirements without altering the clinical logic. The only differences are minor formatting adjustments in the extracted profile text, such as removing the period after 'U07.1' and changing 'should be assigned' to 'should be assigned', which do not affect the coding behavior.
findings.detail:
October 1, 2024 (Older): Assign code U07.1 for newborns testing positive for COVID-19 without documented transmission type. -> October 2025 (New Version): Assign code U07.1 for newborns testing positive for COVID-19 without specific transmission documentation
October 1, 2024 (Older): The appropriate code from category Z38... should be assigned as the principal diagnosis. -> October 2025 (New Version): Assign category Z38 code as principal diagnosis for the birth episode in newborn records

findings.summary: The older version established the fundamental rule that Z38 codes are the principal diagnosis for newborns at birth and must not be used for transfers or maternal records. The newer version retains these core restrictions but expands the guidance by clarifying how to handle perinatal conditions and the combination of codes from other chapters with Chapter 16 codes. This shift moves from a basic exclusion rule to a more detailed operational framework for complex newborn encounters.
findings.detail:
October 1, 2024 (Older): Focuses solely on assigning Z38 as the principal diagnosis and excluding it for transfers or maternal records. -> October 2025 (New Version): Retains Z38 exclusions but adds specific instructions to sequence Chapter 16 codes first for perinatal conditions and allows codes from other chapters if they provide more specific detail.
October 1, 2024 (Older): Does not mention the use of signs and symptoms codes or definitive diagnosis requirements. -> October 2025 (New Version): Explicitly states that codes for signs and symptoms may be assigned when a definitive diagnosis has not been established.

findings.summary: The older version from October 1, 2024, explicitly states that code P95 is restricted to institutions maintaining separate stillbirth records and must not be assigned to the mother's record. The newer version from October 2025 contains the exact same wording and logic, prohibiting concurrent codes and maintaining the restriction to specific institutional record-keeping practices. There are no substantive differences in the coding rules, definitions, or scope between the two versions.
findings.detail: October 1, 2024 (Older): Code P95 is restricted to institutions maintaining separate records for stillbirths. No other code should be used with P95. Code P95 should not be used on the mother's record. -> October 2025 (New Version): Code P95 is restricted to institutions maintaining separate records for stillbirths. No other code should be used with P95. Code P95 should not be used on the mother's record.

findings.summary: The October 1, 2024 guideline and the October 2025 version contain identical text regarding the use of category Z34 codes for uncomplicated prenatal visits. Both versions explicitly state that these codes must be the first-listed diagnosis and prohibit combination with Chapter 15 codes. There are no alterations to the clinical logic, requirements, or behavioral instructions between the two versions.
findings.detail: October 1, 2024 (Older): Use category Z34 codes for routine outpatient prenatal visits when no complications are present. -> October 2025 (New Version): Use category Z34 codes for routine outpatient prenatal visits when no complications are present.

findings.section: Obstetric Coding Sequencing and Provider Responsibility
findings.summary: The October 1, 2024 guideline establishes that Chapter 15 codes (O00-O9A) take sequencing priority and mandates the use of Z33.1 for incidental pregnancies. The October 2025 version repeats these exact instructions without altering the logic, requirements, or specific code assignments. The text excerpts are virtually identical, indicating a maintenance update rather than a policy revision.
findings.detail:
October 1, 2024 (Older): Chapter 15 codes have sequencing priority over codes from other chapters. -> October 2025 (New Version): Chapter 15 codes have sequencing priority over codes from other chapters.
October 1, 2024 (Older): Code Z33.1 is used if the pregnancy is incidental to the encounter, replacing chapter 15 codes. -> October 2025 (New Version): Code Z33.1 is used in place of chapter 15 codes if the pregnancy is incidental to the encounter.

findings.summary: The October 1, 2024 guideline and the October 2025 version present the exact same clinical criteria for assigning codes R03.0, O13.-, and O14.-. There are no substantive differences in the logic, requirements, or behavioral instructions between the two versions; the text is effectively identical.
findings.detail:
October 1, 2024 (Older): Assign code R03.0 for elevated blood pressure reading without a diagnosis of hypertension unless the patient has an established diagnosis of hypertension. -> October 2025 (New Version): Assign code R03.0 for elevated blood pressure reading without a diagnosis of hypertension unless the patient has an established diagnosis of hypertension.
October 1, 2024 (Older): Codes O13.- and O14.- are used for transient hypertension of pregnancy. -> October 2025 (New Version): Assign code O13.-, Gestational [pregnancy-induced] hypertension without significant proteinuria, or O14.-, Pre-eclampsia, for transient hypertension of pregnancy.

findings.summary: The October 1, 2024 and October 2025 versions contain identical text regarding the use of Z34, Z3A, and Z37 codes, including the specific exclusions for abortive outcomes and the requirement for Z34 to be listed first. There are no differences in coding logic, definitions, or application rules between the two versions provided. The text appears to be a static excerpt from the ICD-10-CM guidelines that has not been modified between these two time points in the source data.
findings.detail:
October 1, 2024 (Older): Z codes for pregnancy are for use in those circumstances when none of the problems or complications included in the codes from the Obstetrics chapter exist. -> October 2025 (New Version): Z codes for pregnancy are for use in those circumstances when none of the problems or complications included in the codes from the Obstetrics chapter exist.
October 1, 2024 (Older): Category Z3A codes should not be assigned for pregnancies with abortive outcomes (categories O00O08). -> October 2025 (New Version): Category Z3A codes should not be assigned for pregnancies with abortive outcomes (categories O00O08).

findings.summary: The older version from October 1, 2024, explicitly states that the code may be used at any time after the initial postpartum period. The newer version from October 2025 contains the exact same wording and behavioral instruction. There is no alteration in the coding logic, scope, or definition between the two versions.
findings.detail: October 1, 2024 (Older): This code may be used at any time after the initial postpartum period. -> October 2025 (New Version): This code may be used at any time after the initial postpartum period.

findings.summary: The older and newer guidelines present the exact same text regarding the definition of peripartum cardiomyopathy, specifically the timing of diagnosis and the exclusion of pre-existing heart disease. There are no alterations to the coding rules, requirements, or behavioral instructions between the two versions. The content remains static, indicating a copy or minor metadata update rather than a guideline revision.
findings.detail: October 1, 2024 (Older): Code O90.3 is used for cardiomyopathy diagnosed in the third trimester or progressing months after delivery in patients without pre-existing heart disease. -> October 2025 (New Version): Code O90.3 is used for cardiomyopathy diagnosed in the third trimester or progressing months after delivery in patients without pre-existing heart disease.

findings.summary: The October 1, 2024 version and the October 2025 version contain identical instructions to refer to Section I.C.16 for newborn code usage. While the 2024 version included a detailed list of specific Z codes (Z76.1, Z00.1-, Z38) in the extracted profile, the core behavioral instruction in both texts is simply to direct users to the specific section for further instruction. The 2025 version acts as a navigational reference without altering the underlying coding logic or adding new rules.
findings.detail: October 1, 2024 (Older): Refer to Section I.C.16 for further instruction on newborn code usage, with specific examples of Z76.1, Z00.1-, and Z38 listed in the profile. -> October 2025 (New Version): Refer to Section I.C.16 for further instruction on the use of newborn codes, serving as a navigational reference.

findings.summary: The older version from October 1, 2024 explicitly states that Chapter 15 codes are to be used only on the maternal record and never on the newborn's record. The newer version from October 2025 contains the exact same text and logic, enforcing the same exclusive application to the mother's medical record. There are no alterations to the coding logic, scope, or definitions between the two versions.
findings.detail: October 1, 2024 (Older): Chapter 15 codes are to be used only on the maternal record, never on the record of the newborn. -> October 2025 (New Version): Chapter 15 codes are to be used only on the maternal record, never on the record of the newborn.

findings.summary: The core coding logic remains identical in both versions, mandating O98.5- as the principal diagnosis when COVID-19 is the reason for admission and the primary reason as principal when unrelated. The only variation is a minor wording adjustment in the second version changing 'tests positive' to 'has been diagnosed' and adding 'childbirth' to the pregnancy definition, which does not alter the clinical coding rules.
findings.detail:
October 1, 2024 (Older): Uses the phrase 'tests positive for COVID-19' to trigger additional coding -> October 2025 (New Version): Uses the phrase 'has been diagnosed with COVID-19' to trigger additional coding.
October 1, 2024 (Older): Lists conditions as 'pregnancy, childbirth, or the puerperium' -> October 2025 (New Version): Lists conditions as 'pregnancy, childbirth, or the puerperium' (identical, but New Version explicitly repeats 'childbirth' in the requirements section for emphasis).

findings.summary: The October 1, 2024 guideline and the October 2025 version present the exact same instruction regarding Z37 code assignment. Both versions mandate including Z37 codes on maternal records for deliveries while explicitly prohibiting their use on subsequent or newborn records. The raw text excerpts are identical, indicating that the coding logic has remained static without any clarifications, expansions, or reversals.
findings.detail: October 1, 2024 (Older): A code from category Z37, Outcome of delivery, should be included on every maternal record when a delivery has occurred. These codes are not to be used on subsequent records or on the newborn record. -> October 2025 (New Version): A code from category Z37, Outcome of delivery, should be included on every maternal record when a delivery has occurred. These codes are not to be used on subsequent records or on the newborn record.

findings.summary: The October 1, 2024 guideline explicitly states that the trimester character must be assigned based on when the complication developed, not the discharge trimester. The October 2025 version contains the exact same instruction in the raw text excerpt, indicating no alteration to the clinical logic or coding requirements. Both versions mandate using the trimester of admission/encounter for pre-existing conditions, confirming the rule remains static.
findings.detail: October 1, 2024 (Older): Assign trimester based on complication development time, not discharge time. -> October 2025 (New Version): Assign trimester based on complication development time, not discharge time.

findings.summary: The October 1, 2024 and October 2025 guidelines present the exact same coding logic for malignancy encounters, including the exception for Z51 codes and the prohibition of using symptoms as principal diagnoses. The newer version merely rephrases the sequencing instruction slightly for clarity without altering the underlying clinical criteria or requirements. Both versions mandate that the primary malignancy be the principal diagnosis if treatment targets the primary site, and both require the underlying condition to be secondary when a Z51 code is principal.
findings.detail: October 1, 2024 (Older): Sequence primary site first, followed by any metastatic sites for treatment of primary malignancy. -> October 2025 (New Version): Sequence the primary site first, followed by any metastatic sites when the reason for the encounter is treatment of a primary malignancy.

findings.summary: The older version focused strictly on the binary decision of using a primary malignancy code versus a Z85 history code based on treatment status. The newer version expands this scope by explicitly defining how to code secondary malignant neoplasms (allowing them as principal diagnoses) and detailing specific sequencing rules for encounters involving antineoplastic therapy and brachytherapy.
findings.detail:
October 1, 2024 (Older): Focuses on using primary codes until treatment is completed or Z85 codes if excised with no further treatment. -> October 2025 (New Version): Adds explicit instructions to code secondary malignant neoplasms to the affected site as the principal diagnosis with Z85 as a secondary code.
October 1, 2024 (Older): Mentions Z85 usage for excised malignancies but lacks specific therapy sequencing. -> October 2025 (New Version): Specifies assigning Z51.0, Z51.11, or Z51.12 as principal diagnosis for encounters chiefly for antineoplastic therapy administration.

findings.section: Neoplasm and Anemia Encounter Coding Guidelines
findings.summary: The October 1, 2024 guideline and the October 2025 version contain verbatim identical text regarding the sequencing of malignancy codes versus anemia code D63.0. Both versions explicitly state that when treatment is limited to anemia associated with malignancy, the malignancy must be sequenced first, followed by D63.0. Similarly, both versions mandate that neoplasm complications are coded first if treatment is limited to those complications. There are no differences in logic, requirements, or clinical criteria between the two texts.
findings.detail:
October 1, 2024 (Older): When admission is for management of anemia associated with malignancy, sequence malignancy first followed by D63.0 -> October 2025 (New Version): When admission is for management of anemia associated with malignancy, sequence malignancy first followed by D63.0
October 1, 2024 (Older): When encounter is for management of neoplasm complication, sequence complication first followed by neoplasm -> October 2025 (New Version): When encounter is for management of neoplasm complication, sequence complication first followed by neoplasm

findings.summary: The October 1, 2024 and October 2025 versions contain identical text regarding the protocol for querying providers on multiple tumors in the same organ. There are no alterations to the coding logic, the conditions for querying, or the requirements for documentation clarity between the two versions. The extracted profiles and raw text excerpts are character-for-character consistent, indicating a copy-paste update rather than a substantive guideline revision.
findings.detail: October 1, 2024 (Older): Protocol requires querying for unclear tumor status -> October 2025 (New Version): Protocol requires querying for unclear tumor status (Identical text)

findings.summary: The older version from October 1, 2024, and the new version from October 2025 contain the exact same raw text excerpt regarding the use of code C80.0. Both guidelines strictly define the code for cases with advanced metastatic disease where no primary or secondary sites are known, and both explicitly forbid using it to replace specific site codes. There are no differences in behavior, requirements, or clinical criteria between the two versions.
findings.detail: October 1, 2024 (Older): Code C80.0 is for use only when the patient has advanced metastatic disease and no known primary or secondary sites are specified. -> October 2025 (New Version): Code C80.0 is for use only when the patient has advanced metastatic disease and no known primary or secondary sites are specified.

findings.summary: The older version from October 1, 2024, and the new version from October 2025 contain identical text regarding the usage of code C80.1 for unspecified primary malignancy neoplasms. Both versions explicitly state that the code equates to 'Cancer, unspecified' and must only be used when the primary site cannot be determined. Furthermore, both guidelines maintain the specific restriction that this code should rarely be used in the inpatient setting, indicating no logical or behavioral delta between the two versions.
findings.detail: October 1, 2024 (Older): Code C80.1 equates to Cancer, unspecified and should rarely be used in the inpatient setting. -> October 2025 (New Version): Code C80.1 equates to Cancer, unspecified and should rarely be used in the inpatient setting.

findings.summary: The October 1, 2024 guideline instructs coders to assign C81-C85 codes with the 'extranodal and solid organ sites' character when lymphoid tissue metastasizes, explicitly prohibiting secondary neoplasm codes. The October 2025 version retains this exact logic and example, differing only in the removal of a space within the code string 'C83.39 8' to 'C83.398'. Since the clinical instruction and coding outcome remain identical, this is a formatting correction rather than a rule revision.
findings.detail:
October 1, 2024 (Older): Example code listed as 'C83.39 8' with a space -> October 2025 (New Version): Example code corrected to 'C83.398' without a space.
October 1, 2024 (Older): Logic prohibits secondary neoplasm codes for extranodal metastasis -> October 2025 (New Version): Logic remains identical, prohibiting secondary neoplasm codes.

findings.summary: The October 1, 2024 guideline explicitly states that the secondary neoplasm is the principal diagnosis when treatment targets the secondary site only, even if the primary malignancy is present. The October 2025 version contains the exact same behavioral rule and raw text excerpt, indicating no substantive update to the coding logic. The only variation is a minor grammatical adjustment in the extracted profile summary, which does not alter the clinical instruction.
findings.detail: October 1, 2024 (Older): Secondary neoplasm is designated as the principal diagnosis when treatment is directed toward the secondary site only. -> October 2025 (New Version): Secondary neoplasm is designated as principal diagnosis when treatment is directed toward the secondary site only.

findings.summary: The October 1, 2024 guideline instructs coders to designate the complication as the principal diagnosis when treating a complication from neoplasm surgery, referencing the current versus personal history guideline for secondary codes. The October 2025 version retains this exact logic, merely rephrasing the requirement to ensure documentation supports the complication classification without altering the coding outcome. The core instruction to prioritize the complication and consult the history guideline remains functionally identical in both versions.
findings.detail: October 1, 2024 (Older): Designate the complication as the principal/first-listed diagnosis when an encounter is for treatment of a complication resulting from a surgical procedure performed for the treatment of the neoplasm. -> October 2025 (New Version): Designate the complication resulting from a surgical procedure for neoplasm treatment as the principal/first-listed diagnosis.

findings.summary: The October 1, 2024 guideline and the October 2025 version present the exact same clinical criteria and coding instructions for this feature. The raw text excerpts are identical, indicating that the logic for selecting the principal diagnosis based on treatment direction has not been altered. Any differences in the provided profiles are limited to minor phrasing variations in the summary sections rather than substantive changes to the coding rules.
findings.detail: October 1, 2024 (Older): Designate metastatic site as principal diagnosis when treatment is directed toward it only | Code primary malignancy as an additional code when metastatic site is principal -> October 2025 (New Version): Designate the metastatic site as the principal/first-listed diagnosis when treatment is directed toward it only. | Code the primary malignancy as an additional code when the encounter focuses on the metastatic site.

findings.summary: The October 1, 2024 guideline instructs coders to assign a T86.- complication code followed by C80.2 for malignant neoplasms of transplanted organs. The October 2025 version retains this exact sequence and logic without altering the clinical criteria or code hierarchy. The only variations are minor wording adjustments in the extracted profile requirements, such as changing 'must be used' to 'must be included', which do not affect the operational coding rule.
findings.detail:
October 1, 2024 (Older): Code C80.2 must be used for malignant neoplasm associated with transplanted organ -> October 2025 (New Version): Code C80.2 (Malignant neoplasm associated with transplanted organ) must be included.
October 1, 2024 (Older): Use an additional code for the specific malignancy -> October 2025 (New Version): An additional code specifying the exact malignancy is required.

findings.section: Malignant Neoplasm Sequencing for Pregnant Patients
findings.summary: The older and newer guidelines present the exact same sequencing requirement: code O9A.1- must be sequenced first, followed by a Chapter 2 code. The raw text excerpts are identical in wording and logic, indicating no update to the clinical criteria or coding instructions. The only variation is the metadata identifier (Chunk 116 vs Chunk 543), which does not affect the feature behavior.
findings.detail: October 1, 2024 (Older): Code from subcategory O9A.1- must be sequenced first, followed by the appropriate code from Chapter 2. -> October 2025 (New Version): Code from subcategory O9A.1- must be sequenced first, followed by the appropriate code from Chapter 2.

findings.summary: The October 1, 2024 guideline and the October 2025 version contain verbatim identical text regarding the designation of primary malignancy as the principal diagnosis. There are no alterations in the clinical logic, requirements, or behavioral instructions between the two versions. The only differences are minor formatting artifacts in the extracted profile summaries, which do not affect the actual coding rule.
findings.detail:
October 1, 2024 (Older): Designate primary malignancy or appropriate metastatic site as principal diagnosis when reason for admission is to determine extent of malignancy -> October 2025 (New Version): Designate primary malignancy or appropriate metastatic site as principal diagnosis when reason for admission is to determine extent of malignancy
October 1, 2024 (Older): Maintain primary malignancy or metastatic site as principal diagnosis even when chemotherapy or radiotherapy is administered -> October 2025 (New Version): Apply principal diagnosis designation even when chemotherapy or radiotherapy is administered

findings.summary: The October 1, 2024 and October 2025 guidelines present the exact same coding logic for outpatient first-listed diagnoses, including the requirement to use the Alphabetic Index first and the precedence of ICD-10-CM conventions. The text excerpts are nearly identical, with the 2025 version only adding a minor clarification about differentiating outpatient practices from inpatient settings regarding abnormal findings. No substantive changes to the coding behavior or requirements were observed between the two versions.
findings.detail: October 1, 2024 (Older): Defines the use of first-listed diagnosis and mandates starting code assignment searches in the Alphabetic Index. -> October 2025 (New Version): Defines the same use of first-listed diagnosis and mandates the same search order, with an added note to differentiate outpatient coding from inpatient practices regarding abnormal findings.

findings.summary: The core coding logic remains identical in both versions, explicitly requiring T86.1 only for documented complications like failure or rejection while excluding uncomplicated CKD. The newer version retains the exact same instructions for querying providers on unclear documentation and referencing section I.C.14 for non-complicated cases. While the newer version includes a specific reference to section I.C.19 for organ trauma in its taxonomy description, the extracted behavioral profiles and raw text excerpts are functionally equivalent with no substantive logic delta.
findings.detail: October 1, 2024 (Older): Refer to section I.C.1.d.4 for specific sequencing instructions regarding sepsis due to infection in a transplanted organ. -> October 2025 (New Version): Refer to section I.C.1.d.4 for specific sequencing instructions regarding sepsis due to infection in a transplanted organ.

findings.summary: The older guideline focused primarily on assigning B20 for documented HIV disease and Z79.899 for medication use. The newer version expands the scope by explicitly defining rules for inconclusive serology (R75), asymptomatic status (Z21), and establishing strict sequencing priorities for pregnancy cases (O98.7) to prevent incorrect code assignment.
findings.detail:
October 1, 2024 (Older): Focuses on assigning B20 for documented HIV disease and Z79.899 for long-term drug therapy without specific sequencing rules for pregnancy. -> October 2025 (New Version): Expands to include specific sequencing priorities where O98.7 takes priority over B20 or Z21 in pregnancy cases and defines rules for inconclusive serology (R75).
October 1, 2024 (Older): Does not mention coding for inconclusive HIV serology or asymptomatic status distinctions. -> October 2025 (New Version): Explicitly assigns R75 for inconclusive serology and Z21 for asymptomatic status only if no HIV disease is documented.

findings.summary: The older version provided general guidelines stating Z codes could be used as first-listed or secondary codes based on encounter circumstances. The newer version clarifies this by explicitly discouraging the use of non-specific Z codes in inpatient settings and limiting outpatient use to instances where no further documentation permits more precise coding. It further mandates that any sign, symptom, or reason for visit captured in another code must be used instead of a non-specific Z code, and provides specific deprecated mappings for administrative examinations.
findings.detail:
October 1, 2024 (Older): Z codes may be used as either a first-listed or secondary code depending on the circumstances of the encounter. -> October 2025 (New Version): Use of non-specific Z codes in the inpatient setting is discouraged due to redundancy with other codes, and outpatient use is limited to instances where no further documentation permits more precise coding.
October 1, 2024 (Older): No specific restrictions on non-specific codes mentioned. -> October 2025 (New Version): Any sign, symptom, or reason for visit captured in another code should be used instead of a non-specific Z code, with specific deprecation of Z02.9 in favor of Z04.9, Z13.9, or Z41.9.

findings.summary: The October 1, 2024 guideline instructs coders to assign N18 codes for residual CKD and Z94.0 for transplant status, explicitly stating that CKD alone is not a complication. The October 2025 version contains the exact same text and logic, reinforcing that specific documentation of complications like rejection or failure is required for separate coding. There are no alterations in the coding rules, definitions, or procedural steps between the two versions.
findings.detail: October 1, 2024 (Older): Assign N18 for CKD stage and Z94.0 for transplant status; do not code CKD as complication unless specific complication documented -> October 2025 (New Version): Assign N18 for CKD stage and Z94.0 for transplant status; do not code CKD as complication unless specific complication documented

findings.summary: The older guideline strictly limited the mapping logic to Z28 encounters, explicitly excluding Z28.3 from mapping to Z29, Z40, and Z41. The newer version retains these Z28 rules but significantly expands the scope by introducing a new mapping set for Z76.3 encounters, covering scenarios like 'boarder to healthcare facility' and 'patient noncompliance'. This addition requires coders to verify if the source code is Z28 or Z76.3 before applying the respective target codes.
findings.detail:
October 1, 2024 (Older): Mapping was restricted exclusively to Z28 encounters (Z29, Z40, Z41) with specific exclusions for Z28.3 -> October 2025 (New Version): Mapping now includes Z28 encounters plus a new set for Z76.3 encounters (Z76.4, Z76.5, Z91.1-).
October 1, 2024 (Older): Requirements focused on excluding Z28.3 from Z29/Z40/Z41 mappings -> October 2025 (New Version): Requirements now mandate verifying the source code is either Z28 or Z76.3 and checking specific subcode exclusions for both categories.

findings.summary: The October 1, 2024 and October 2025 guidelines present the exact same definitions for screening versus diagnostic examinations, including the requirement for a procedure code to confirm the screening. The rules regarding first-listed versus additional codes and the handling of incidental findings discovered during screening are identical in both texts. The only differences are minor formatting artifacts in the raw text excerpts, such as the truncation of the Z11/Z12 category list, which do not affect the coding logic.
findings.detail: October 1, 2024 (Older): The Z code indicates that a screening exam is planned and a procedure code is required to confirm it. -> October 2025 (New Version): The Z code indicates that a screening exam is planned and a procedure code is required to confirm it.

findings.summary: The October 1, 2024 guideline explicitly states that Z05 codes must be secondary to Z38 codes for liveborn infants. The October 2025 version repeats this exact instruction without altering the logic, requirements, or sequencing rules. The only differences are minor phrasing adjustments in the extracted profile summaries, while the core coding behavior remains identical.
findings.detail:
October 1, 2024 (Older): Z05 codes must be assigned as secondary codes following a Z38 code -> October 2025 (New Version): Z05 codes must be assigned as secondary codes following a Z38 code
October 1, 2024 (Older): A condition from category Z05 must be documented in addition to the birth circumstance -> October 2025 (New Version): The place of birth or type of delivery must be documented to justify the Z38 code

findings.summary: The October 1, 2024 guideline explicitly states that Z codes are not procedure codes and require a corresponding procedure code to describe any performed action. The October 2025 version contains the exact same text and logic, indicating no substantive update to the coding requirement. The taxonomy descriptions and extracted behaviors are identical across both versions, confirming the rule remains static.
findings.detail: October 1, 2024 (Older): Z codes are not procedure codes. A corresponding procedure code must accompany a Z code to describe any procedure performed. -> October 2025 (New Version): Z codes are not procedure codes. A corresponding procedure code must accompany a Z code to describe any procedure performed.

findings.section: Z Code Guidelines for Specified Encounters
findings.summary: The older version from October 1, 2024, and the new version from October 2025 contain the exact same raw text excerpt regarding chapter-specific guidelines for Z codes. There are no differences in the coding logic, scope, or definitions provided in the excerpts. The text appears to be a static note without any updates or clarifications in this specific instance.
findings.detail: October 1, 2024 (Older): Note: The chapter specific guidelines provide additional information about the use of Z codes for specified encounters. -> October 2025 (New Version): Note: The chapter specific guidelines provide additional information about the use of Z codes for specified encounters.

findings.summary: The October 1, 2024 guideline and the October 2025 version present the exact same behavioral rules for coding abnormal findings during general medical examinations. Both versions mandate that the examination code with abnormal findings be the first-listed diagnosis and require a secondary code for the specific condition. The raw text excerpts are identical, indicating no substantive update to the clinical criteria or coding hierarchy.
findings.detail: October 1, 2024 (Older): Mandates assigning the examination with abnormal findings as the first-listed diagnosis when a condition is newly identified or severity changes. -> October 2025 (New Version): Mandates assigning the examination with abnormal findings as the first-listed diagnosis when a condition is newly identified or severity changes.

findings.summary: The October 1, 2024 guideline instructs coders to assign Z29.81 for PrEP administration and to code any documented risk factors. The October 2025 version retains this identical logic, merely rephrasing the requirement to code risk factors as 'in addition to the primary encounter code' without altering the clinical criteria. The definition of PrEP and the target population remain unchanged between the two versions.
findings.detail: October 1, 2024 (Older): Code any documented risk factors for HIV in addition to the primary encounter code. -> October 2025 (New Version): Code any documented risk factors for HIV in addition to the primary encounter code.

findings.summary: The older version from October 1, 2024 explicitly mandates the use of codes from ranges A00.0 through T88.9, Z00-Z99, and U00-U85 to identify diagnoses and reasons for encounters. The newer version from October 2025 retains this exact requirement without altering the specific code ranges or the logic for their application. The only differences are minor formatting adjustments in the 'Requirements' section, such as changing 'specific range' to 'specified ranges' and splitting the list, which do not impact the coding logic.
findings.detail: October 1, 2024 (Older): Diagnosis codes must be selected from the specific range A00.0 through T88.9, Z00-Z99, and U00-U85. -> October 2025 (New Version): Diagnosis codes must be selected from the specified ranges: A00.0 through T88.9, Z00-Z99, and U00-U85.

findings.summary: The October 1, 2024 version explicitly excluded Z71.84 and Z71.85 from the general counseling category due to their specific high-risk contexts. The October 2025 version clarifies the handling of Z71 codes by distinguishing general health counseling from specific travel risk counseling, while simultaneously introducing a new rule to map mental health services for abuse victims and perpetrators to contraception counseling codes.
findings.detail:
October 1, 2024 (Older): Z71.84 and Z71.85 are excluded from the general counseling category because they represent specific high-risk scenarios requiring distinct clinical contexts. -> October 2025 (New Version): The system distinguishes between general health counseling (Z71) and specific travel risk counseling (Z71.84) to ensure accurate coding for future travel purposes.
October 1, 2024 (Older): No mention of mapping mental health services for abuse to contraception codes. -> October 2025 (New Version): Encounters involving mental health services for victims and perpetrators of abuse are mapped under contraception counseling codes, treating them as a unified counseling session.

findings.summary: The older version (October 1, 2024) and the newer version (October 2025) contain identical raw text excerpts regarding the sequencing of chemotherapy encounters. Both guidelines explicitly state that the diagnosis chiefly responsible for services is sequenced first, with the specific exception that the Z code is listed first only when chemotherapy or radiation is the primary reason. The extracted behaviors and requirements are functionally equivalent, indicating the text was likely updated for formatting or minor wording without altering the coding logic.
findings.detail: October 1, 2024 (Older): Sequence the diagnosis chiefly responsible first, except list Z code first if chemotherapy/radiation is primary -> October 2025 (New Version): Sequence the diagnosis chiefly responsible first, except list Z code first if chemotherapy/radiation is primary

findings.summary: The older guideline broadly discouraged the use of non-specific Z codes in the inpatient setting to prevent redundancy with more precise codes. The newer version expands the scope by explicitly defining which specific Z code categories (Z00, Z01, Z02, Z04) are restricted from being reported as the principal diagnosis, while introducing specific exceptions for combined encounters on the same day and mapping specific infertility codes.
findings.detail:
October 1, 2024 (Older): Certain non-specific Z codes are generally discouraged in the inpatient setting due to redundancy with other codes. -> October 2025 (New Version): Specific Z code categories (Z00, Z01, Z02, Z04) are restricted from being the principal diagnosis, except when multiple encounters on the same day are combined.
October 1, 2024 (Older): Focuses on avoiding non-specific codes when more precise documentation exists. -> October 2025 (New Version): Introduces explicit mappings for infertility scenarios (e.g., Z31.81 to Z31.83 or Z31.84) and pregnancy supervision exceptions.

findings.summary: The October 1, 2024 guideline and the October 2025 version present the exact same instructions regarding the application of Z80-Z87 codes. Both versions mandate coding only for conditions coexisting at the time of the encounter that affect care, while explicitly excluding previously resolved conditions. The raw text excerpts are identical, indicating that the guideline text was likely updated for formatting or minor wording without altering the underlying coding logic.
findings.detail: October 1, 2024 (Older): Code all documented conditions that coexist at the time of the encounter/visit and that require or affect patient care, treatment or management. -> October 2025 (New Version): Code all documented conditions that coexist at the time of the encounter/visit and that require or affect patient care, treatment or management.

findings.summary: The October 1, 2024 guideline and the October 2025 version present the exact same instructions for assigning Z79.4, Z79.84, and Z79.85 codes based on medication combinations. The only variation is the removal of the specific exclusion rule regarding temporary insulin use in the 2025 version, which appears to be a formatting or extraction artifact rather than a substantive change to the core coding logic. The fundamental requirement that patients must routinely use the medications for long-term care remains identical in both texts.
findings.detail:
October 1, 2024 (Older): Explicitly states 'Do not assign code Z79.4 if insulin is given temporarily' -> October 2025 (New Version): Omits this specific exclusion sentence, though the general requirement for 'routinely use' implies the same restriction.
October 1, 2024 (Older): Lists requirements as a distinct section with three bullet points -> October 2025 (New Version): Condenses requirements into a single sentence within the behaviors list.

findings.section: O80 Delivery Code Usage for Z37.0
findings.summary: The October 1, 2024 guideline explicitly restricted O80 to Z37.0, stating it is the only appropriate outcome code. The October 2025 version contains the exact same text and logic, confirming that the rule remains unchanged between versions.
findings.detail: October 1, 2024 (Older): Z37.0 is the only outcome of delivery code appropriate for use with O80. -> October 2025 (New Version): Z37.0 is the only outcome of delivery code appropriate for use with O80.

findings.summary: The older version from October 1, 2024, and the new version from October 2025 contain the exact same raw text excerpt regarding miscellaneous Z codes. The behaviors listed for both versions are identical, indicating that the coding logic and guidelines for this specific feature have not been altered between these two timeframes.
findings.detail: October 1, 2024 (Older): The miscellaneous Z codes capture a number of other health care encounters that do not fall into one of the other categories. -> October 2025 (New Version): The miscellaneous Z codes capture a number of other health care encounters that do not fall into one of the other categories.

findings.summary: The October 1, 2024 and October 2025 guidelines present the exact same coding logic for Observation Z codes, including the restriction against use when symptoms are present and the specific sequencing rules for Z38 and Z03.7 codes. While the New Version text includes slight phrasing variations (e.g., adding 'must' to the Z03.7 restriction and repeating the fetal condition instruction), the clinical criteria and resulting coding behavior remain identical to the Older version. There are no substantive changes to the rules governing when these codes can or cannot be assigned.
findings.detail:
October 1, 2024 (Older): Codes from subcategory Z03.7 are not for use in encounters for antenatal screening of mother. -> October 2025 (New Version): Codes from subcategory Z03.7 must not be used for encounters for antenatal screening of mother.
October 1, 2024 (Older): For suspected fetal conditions that are inconclusive, assign codes from categories O35, O36, O40, or O41 instead of Z codes. -> October 2025 (New Version): For encounters for suspected fetal conditions that are inconclusive following testing and evaluation, codes from categories O35, O36, O40, or O41 must be assigned instead of Z codes.

findings.summary: The October 1, 2024 guideline instructs coders to sequence Z01.81 first for preoperative evaluations only, followed by the reason for surgery and any findings. The October 2025 version repeats this exact logic with identical wording regarding the Z01.81 subcategory and the requirement to assign additional codes for the surgical condition and findings. There are no alterations to the clinical criteria, sequencing rules, or code assignments between the two versions.
findings.detail: October 1, 2024 (Older): Sequence first Z01.81 for pre-op evaluations only, then assign additional codes for surgery reason and findings. -> October 2025 (New Version): Sequence first Z01.81 for pre-op evaluations only, then assign additional codes for surgery reason and findings.

findings.section: Postpartum and Neoplasm Follow-up Code Assignments
findings.summary: The October 1, 2024 guideline and the October 2025 version present the exact same requirements for assigning codes Z08 and Z09 based on completed treatment modalities. The only observable difference is a minor formatting adjustment in the Z39 section where a hyphen was added in the newer version, but this does not alter the clinical coding logic or definitions. Both versions strictly require that treatment for malignant or non-malignant conditions be completed before these follow-up codes can be assigned.
findings.detail: October 1, 2024 (Older): Z39 listed as a standalone header -> October 2025 (New Version): Z39 listed with a preceding hyphen for formatting consistency.

findings.summary: The October 1, 2024 guideline and the October 2025 version contain identical text regarding the assignment of code A92.5 based solely on provider confirmation without test documentation. Both versions explicitly prohibit using A92.5 for 'suspected', 'possible', or 'probable' cases and mandate the use of symptom codes or Z20.821 for non-confirmed diagnoses. The raw text excerpts are verbatim matches, indicating that the coding logic has remained static between these two releases.
findings.detail: October 1, 2024 (Older): Code only a confirmed diagnosis of Zika virus (A92.5) as documented by the provider. -> October 2025 (New Version): Code only a confirmed diagnosis of Zika virus (A92.5) as documented by the provider.

findings.summary: The older version explicitly defines guidelines for category Z05 codes regarding healthy newborns and readmissions, which is unrelated to the Z79 feature. The newer version provides detailed criteria for assigning Z79 codes for long-term prophylactic or chronic medication use and specific rules for tPA transfer reporting. Since the older text contains no information regarding Z79 codes, there is no meaningful delta to analyze between the two versions for this specific feature.
findings.detail: October 1, 2024 (Older): Describes Z05 code assignment for healthy newborns -> October 2025 (New Version): Describes Z79 code assignment for chronic medications and tPA transfers

findings.summary: The October 1, 2024 guideline and the October 2025 version present the exact same coding logic for Z52, specifying that codes apply to living individuals donating blood or tissue for others or self. Both versions explicitly exclude cadaveric donations and maintain identical requirements regarding the donor's living status and the nature of the tissue. The text excerpts are virtually identical, indicating that the guideline was updated for formatting or minor wording without altering the clinical coding rules.
findings.detail: October 1, 2024 (Older): Codes in category Z52 are used for living individuals who are donating blood or other body tissue. -> October 2025 (New Version): Codes in category Z52 are used for living individuals who are donating blood or other body tissue.

findings.summary: The older version from October 1, 2024, and the new version from October 2025 contain identical instructions regarding the assignment of Z16 codes for antibiotic-resistant infections. The raw text excerpts are character-for-character the same, indicating no update to the coding logic, definitions, or scope occurred between these versions.
findings.detail: October 1, 2024 (Older): Assign a code from category Z16 following the infection code only if the infection code does not identify drug resistance. -> October 2025 (New Version): Assign a code from category Z16 following the infection code only if the infection code does not identify drug resistance.

findings.summary: The October 1, 2024 and October 2025 guidelines present identical coding logic for aftercare Z codes, including the exclusion of acute diseases and injuries, and the specific sequencing rules for neoplasm therapy. The text in the new version is a slightly condensed and rephrased summary of the older version's detailed requirements without altering any clinical criteria or sequencing mandates. Both versions explicitly state that Z51.0 and Z51.1 codes take precedence over diagnosis codes for neoplasm treatment, and both prohibit using aftercare codes for acute injuries.
findings.detail:
October 1, 2024 (Older): Detailed requirements for multiple antineoplastic therapies (Z51.0 + Z51.1) and specific exclusion of acute disease treatment. -> October 2025 (New Version): Condensed summary of the same requirements, omitting the specific detail about multiple therapy sequencing but retaining the core rule.
October 1, 2024 (Older): Explicit instruction that aftercare codes are generally first listed unless a diagnosis code is applicable. -> October 2025 (New Version): Restates the general sequencing rule with an example of colostomy closure but does not change the underlying logic.

findings.summary: The October 1, 2024 guideline and the October 2025 version present the exact same sequencing rules for pathological fractures, prioritizing M84.5 based on treatment focus and ordering multiple fractures by severity. The text excerpts are verbatim identical, indicating that the coding logic has remained static between these versions. There are no new conditions, definitions, or clarifications introduced in the newer version.
findings.detail:
October 1, 2024 (Older): Sequence M84.5 code first when the focus of treatment is the fracture due to neoplasm -> October 2025 (New Version): Sequence M84.5 code first when the focus of treatment is the fracture due to neoplasm
October 1, 2024 (Older): Sequence multiple fractures based on the severity of the fracture -> October 2025 (New Version): Sequence multiple fractures based on severity

findings.summary: The older version from October 1, 2024, and the new version from October 2025 contain identical text regarding the exemption logic for congenital anomalies. Both guidelines explicitly state that categories Q00-Q99 are on the exempt list and do not receive the 'Y' assignment, while other congenital conditions are always considered present on admission. There are no differences in behavior, requirements, or raw text excerpts between the two versions.
findings.detail: October 1, 2024 (Older): Assign 'Y' for congenital conditions and anomalies except for categories Q00Q99, Congenital anomalies, which are on the exempt list. -> October 2025 (New Version): Assign 'Y' for congenital conditions and anomalies except for categories Q00Q99, Congenital anomalies, which are on the exempt list.

findings.summary: The core logic remains identical: newborns are not admitted until after birth, so conditions present at birth or developed in utero are treated as present at admission. Both versions explicitly state that conditions occurring during delivery (e.g., injury, meconium aspiration) must be assigned 'Y'. The only difference is the addition of a redundant 'Requirements' bullet in the 2025 version reiterating the 'Y' assignment already present in the text.
findings.detail: October 1, 2024 (Older): Logic defined in text and behaviors -> October 2025 (New Version): Logic defined in text, behaviors, and an added redundant requirements bullet.

findings.section: POA Assignment Logic for Obstetrical Conditions
findings.summary: The October 1, 2024 guideline and the October 2025 version contain identical text regarding the assignment of POA indicators for obstetrical conditions. Both versions explicitly state that delivery timing does not affect the indicator and rely solely on the presence of the condition at admission. There are no logical, behavioral, or textual differences between the two provided chunks.
findings.detail: October 1, 2024 (Older): Whether or not the patient delivers during the current hospitalization does not affect assignment of the POA indicator. -> October 2025 (New Version): Whether or not the patient delivers during the current hospitalization does not affect assignment of the POA indicator.

findings.summary: The October 1, 2024 guideline defines a postpartum complication strictly as any complication occurring within the six-week period following delivery. The October 2025 version retains this exact definition without altering the time window, the scope of adverse events, or the classification logic. There are no substantive differences in the coding rules or clinical criteria between the two versions.
findings.detail: October 1, 2024 (Older): A postpartum complication is any complication occurring within the sixweek period. -> October 2025 (New Version): A postpartum complication is any complication occurring within the sixweek period.

findings.summary: The October 1, 2024 guideline defines the postpartum period as six weeks and the peripartum period as the last month of pregnancy through five months postpartum. The October 2025 version repeats these exact same definitions without altering the timeframes or coding logic. There is no delta in the clinical criteria or application rules between the two versions.
findings.detail: October 1, 2024 (Older): Postpartum is six weeks; Peripartum is last month of pregnancy to five months postpartum. -> October 2025 (New Version): Postpartum is six weeks; Peripartum is last month of pregnancy to five months postpartum.

findings.summary: The older version from October 1, 2024, and the new version from October 2025 present the exact same sequencing logic: O9A.2 first, followed by the specific toxic effect code, then the resulting condition code. The raw text excerpts are identical in both chunks, indicating that the guideline text was likely updated for formatting or minor wording rather than altering the coding logic. There are no behavioral deltas to report as the instructions remain functionally unchanged.
findings.detail: October 1, 2024 (Older): Code from subcategory O9A.2 must be sequenced first, followed by the appropriate injury, poisoning, toxic effect, adverse effect or underdosing code, and then the additional code(s) that specifies the condition caused by the poisoning, toxic effect, adverse effect or underdosing. -> October 2025 (New Version): Code from subcategory O9A.2 must be sequenced first, followed by the appropriate injury, poisoning, toxic effect, adverse effect or underdosing code, and then the additional code(s) that specifies the condition caused by the poisoning, toxic effect, adverse effect or underdosing.

findings.section: Anemia Sequencing for Chemotherapy Adverse Effects
findings.summary: The October 1, 2024 guideline and the October 2025 version present the exact same sequencing rules, requiring the anemia code to be listed first followed by neoplasm and adverse effect codes. The raw text excerpts are identical in both versions, indicating no substantive update to the clinical logic or coding requirements. While the extracted profile lists slightly more granular requirements in the new version, the core behavioral instructions and raw source text remain unchanged.
findings.detail:
October 1, 2024 (Older): Sequence anemia code first when admission is for management of anemia associated with chemotherapy/immunotherapy adverse effects and only treatment is for anemia. -> October 2025 (New Version): Sequence anemia code first when admission is for management of anemia associated with chemotherapy adverse effect and only treatment is for anemia.
October 1, 2024 (Older): Follow anemia code with appropriate neoplasm code and adverse effect code (T45.1X5-) for chemotherapy/immunotherapy cases. -> October 2025 (New Version): Follow anemia code with appropriate neoplasm code and adverse effect code (T45.1X5- for chemotherapy) or cause code (Y84.2 for radiotherapy).
October 1, 2024 (Older): Use code Y84.2 specifically for radiotherapy as the cause of abnormal reaction without mention of misadventure. -> October 2025 (New Version): Use Y84.2 specifically for radiotherapy as the cause of abnormal reaction without mention of misadventure.
October 1, 2024 (Older): Raw text excerpt specifies 'chemotherapy or immunotherapy' and 'radiotherapy'. -> October 2025 (New Version): Raw text excerpt specifies 'chemotherapy or immunotherapy' and 'radiotherapy' (identical text).
